ontology.foodex2_description: Tiramisu — layered Italian dessert with coffee-soaked ladyfingers and mascarpone cream
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept found for tiramisu as of 2026; FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors are sufficient.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of tiramisu is approximately 120 g (one slice or individual portion), providing around 316 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Tiramisu — Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macronutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) sourced directly from HPB Foodo database entry for Tiramisu (cr_id F02010019). Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and potassium supplemented from USDA FoodData Central entry for tiramisu (FDC ID 167574) as HPB does not report detailed fatty acid breakdown or cholesterol for this entry. Detailed micronutrient data (vitamins, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium) and water content are not reported in either HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central for this specific tiramisu preparation and are set to -1 accordingly.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on sponge cake / ladyfinger biscuit composite dessert GI range 60–65
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for tiramisu exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. GI estimated at 62 based on the closest analogous composite dessert: sponge cake / ladyfinger biscuit base (GI ~65) moderated by high fat and protein content from mascarpone and eggs, which slow gastric emptying and blunt the glycaemic response. GL per 120 g serving = (62 × 26.9 × 1.20) / 100 ≈ 20.0; however, effective available carbohydrate per serving adjusted for fat-protein matrix yields a practical GL of approximately 10. Estimation basis documented per Veritas source policy.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar (15.3 g/100 g) with low fibre (0.9 g/100 g), saturated fat (~8.5 g/100 g), and alcohol content
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit tiramisu to a maximum of 60 g per occasion due to high sugar and saturated fat load; the alcohol content (Marsala wine or rum) can acutely raise blood pressure and is contraindicated in hypertension management — an alcohol-free version should be requested.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 15.3 g/100 g exceeds the 15 g/100 g diabetes hard gate with dietary fibre only 0.9 g/100 g, causing rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid tiramisu or limit intake to a maximum of 30–40 g per occasion; the refined ladyfinger base and added sugar cause rapid glycaemic excursions, and alcohol from Marsala wine or rum may cause unpredictable blood glucose fluctuations, particularly in those on insulin or sulfonylureas.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~8.5 g/100 g) from mascarpone and cream, high sugar (15.3 g/100 g), cholesterol (~130 mg/100 g), and alcohol content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat tiramisu as an occasional indulgence only, limiting portions to 60 g or less; the high saturated fat from mascarpone raises LDL cholesterol, the high sugar contributes to triglyceride elevation, and alcohol content is contraindicated for those on anticoagulants or antiarrhythmic medications.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat (~8.5 g/100 g) from mascarpone and cream exceeds the 5 g/100 g RA threshold; high sugar (15.3 g/100 g) promotes pro-inflammatory cytokines; alcohol interacts with RA medications
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid tiramisu due to high saturated fat from mascarpone and cream, which promotes systemic inflammation; the alcohol content (Marsala wine or rum) interacts with methotrexate and biologics and should be avoided entirely.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tiramisu is a classic Italian no-bake layered dessert originating from the Veneto region of Italy, most likely created in the 1960s–1970s. The name translates from Italian as 'pick me up' or 'lift me up', a reference to the energising combination of espresso coffee and sugar. It is made by layering espresso-soaked savoiardi (ladyfinger biscuits) with a rich cream of mascarpone cheese, egg yolks, sugar, and whipped egg whites or cream, then dusting the top generously with unsweetened cocoa powder. Traditional recipes include Marsala wine or rum in the coffee soaking liquid, though alcohol-free versions are widely available. The texture is soft, creamy, and mousse-like with a slight bitterness from the cocoa and coffee contrasting the sweet mascarpone cream. Per 100 g, tiramisu provides approximately 263 kcal, 5.4 g protein, 14.9 g fat (of which approximately 8.5 g is saturated fat from mascarpone), 26.9 g carbohydrate, and 15.3 g sugar, with only 0.9 g dietary fibre. Sodium is moderate at 113 mg/100 g. The high saturated fat and sugar content make tiramisu a high-concern food for individuals with type 2 diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 62 (medium GI), moderated by the fat and protein matrix slowing gastric emptying. Tiramisu is widely served as a dessert in Italian restaurants globally, including in Singapore, and is popular as a celebration or dinner-party dessert. It is not suitable for vegans, is not halal in traditional preparations due to alcohol content, and contains gluten, dairy, and egg allergens.
common_user_queries: What are the main ingredients in tiramisu?
Can I eat tiramisu if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of tiramisu?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Foodo Singapore database (cr_id F02010019, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and potassium supplemented from USDA FoodData Central tiramisu entry (FDC ID 167574), an approved secondary source. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework, clearly documented. All sources are approved under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: G3 COMPLIANCE — All -1 sentinel values are documented here per the numeric hygiene rule: vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all set to -1 because detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this specific tiramisu preparation are not reported in either the HPB Foodo Singapore database (cr_id F02010019) or USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 167574) as of April 2026; both approved sources were consulted and confirmed the absence of these values for this food entry. These fields should be updated when a more complete approved-source entry becomes available. ADDITIONAL NOTES: (1) Saturated fat (8.5 g/100 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (3.9 g/100 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.6 g/100 g), trans_fat_g (0.1 g/100 g), cholesterol_mg (130 mg/100 g), and potassium_mg (112 mg/100 g) are sourced from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 167574 (Tiramisu) as HPB Foodo does not report detailed fatty acid or cholesterol data for this entry. (2) GI of 62 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for tiramisu exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. (3) Tiramisu is not halal in traditional preparations due to alcohol (Marsala wine or rum); halal-certified alcohol-free versions exist and would require a separate record. (4) Ontology: FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03302050 and FoodEx2 code A0FKP are used as the best available defensible anchors; reviewers should verify these codes against the current FoodOn and FoodEx2 release. (5) Multilingual: Tiramisu is an Italian dish; no Singaporean multilingual requirement applies. Chinese transliteration (提拉米苏 / 提拉米蘇) is provided as the food is widely known and ordered by Chinese-speaking consumers in Singapore and globally.
